section.title: The Third Journey: Ephesus
section.sectionRationale: The third journey centres on Ephesus, a great city devoted to the goddess Artemis and steeped in magic. Apollos is instructed more accurately, disciples who knew only John's baptism receive the Spirit, and for two years the word spreads from the hall of Tyrannus until all Asia has heard. The gospel exposes counterfeit power in the failure of the sons of Sceva, purges the magic arts, and finally collides with the pagan economy in the silversmiths' riot.
section.thesis: The name of the Lord Jesus, proclaimed and honoured through the Spirit's power, overthrows every rival, the half-knowledge of John's disciples, the counterfeit exorcism of Sceva's sons, the occult books of Ephesus, and the idol trade of Artemis, so that the word of the Lord prevails even against a city's religion and wealth.
section.frameNote: Five movements: the eloquent Apollos taught the way more accurately; the Ephesian disciples who receive the Spirit; two years of preaching from the hall of Tyrannus; the seven sons of Sceva and the burning of the magic books; and the riot of the silversmiths for the sake of Artemis.

units.label: Apollos taught more accurately
units.note: an eloquent man, fervent in spirit

verses.text: After Paul had spent some time in Antioch, he traveled from place to place throughout the region of Galatia and Phrygia, strengthening all the disciples.
Meanwhile a Jew named Apollos, a native of Alexandria, came to Ephesus. He was an eloquent man, well versed in the Scriptures.
He had been instructed in the way of the Lord and was fervent in spirit. He spoke and taught accurately about Jesus, though he knew only the baptism of John.
And he began to speak boldly in the synagogue. When Priscilla and Aquila heard him, they took him aside and explained to him the way of God more accurately.
When Apollos resolved to cross over to Achaia, the brothers encouraged him and wrote to the disciples there to welcome him. On his arrival, he was a great help to those who by grace had believed.
For he powerfully refuted the Jews in public debate, proving from the Scriptures that Jesus is the Christ.

units.label: The Holy Spirit received at Ephesus
units.note: disciples who knew only John's baptism

verses.text: While Apollos was at Corinth, Paul passed through the interior and came to Ephesus. There he found some disciples
and asked them, “Did you receive the Holy Spirit when you became believers?” “No,” they answered, “we have not even heard that there is a Holy Spirit.”
“Into what, then, were you baptized?” Paul asked. “The baptism of John,” they replied.
Paul explained: “John’s baptism was a baptism of {{metanoia|repentance}}. He told the people to believe in the One coming after him, that is, in Jesus.”
On hearing this, they were baptized into the name of the Lord Jesus.
And when Paul laid his hands on them, the Holy Spirit came upon them, and they spoke in tongues and prophesied.
There were about twelve men in all.

units.label: The hall of Tyrannus
units.note: all Asia hears the word of the Lord

verses.text: Then Paul went into the synagogue and spoke boldly there for three months, arguing persuasively about the kingdom of God.

But when some of them stubbornly refused to believe and publicly maligned the {{hodos|Way}}, Paul took his disciples and left the synagogue to conduct daily discussions in the lecture hall of Tyrannus.
This continued for two years, so that everyone who lived in the province of Asia, Jews and Greeks alike, heard the word of the Lord.
God did extraordinary {{dynameis|miracles}} through the hands of Paul,
so that even handkerchiefs and aprons that had touched him were taken to the sick, and the diseases and evil spirits left them.

units.label: The seven sons of Sceva
units.note: counterfeit power exposed, the name honoured, the magic burned

verses.text: Now there were some itinerant Jewish exorcists who tried to invoke the name of the Lord Jesus over those with evil spirits. They would say, “I command you by Jesus, whom Paul proclaims.”
Seven sons of Sceva, a Jewish chief priest, were doing this.
But one day the evil spirit responded, “Jesus I know, and I know about Paul, but who are you?”
Then the man with the evil spirit jumped on them and overpowered them all. The attack was so violent that they ran out of the house naked and wounded.
This became known to all the Jews and Greeks living in Ephesus, and fear came over all of them. So the name of the Lord Jesus was held in high honor.
Many who had believed now came forward, confessing and disclosing their deeds.
And a number of those who had practiced magic arts brought their books and burned them in front of everyone. When the value of the books was calculated, the total came to fifty thousand drachmas.
So the {{logos|word}} of the Lord powerfully continued to spread and prevail.

units.label: The riot of the silversmiths
units.note: Great is Artemis of the Ephesians

verses.text: After these things had happened, Paul resolved in the Spirit to go to Jerusalem, passing through Macedonia and Achaia. “After I have been there,” he said, “I must see Rome as well.”
He sent two of his helpers, Timothy and Erastus, to Macedonia, while he stayed for a time in the province of Asia.
About that time there arose a great disturbance about the Way.
It began with a silversmith named Demetrius who made silver shrines of Artemis, bringing much business to the craftsmen.
Demetrius assembled the craftsmen, along with the workmen in related trades. “Men,” he said, “you know that this business is our source of prosperity.

And you can see and hear that not only in Ephesus, but in nearly the whole province of Asia, this Paul has persuaded a great number of people to turn away. He says that man-made gods are no gods at all.

There is danger not only that our business will fall into disrepute, but also that the temple of the great goddess Artemis will be discredited and her majesty deposed, she who is worshiped by all the province of Asia and the whole world.”
When the men heard this, they were enraged and began shouting, “Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!”
Soon the whole city was in disarray. They rushed together into the theatre, dragging with them Gaius and Aristarchus, Paul’s traveling companions from Macedonia.
Paul wanted to go before the assembly, but the disciples would not allow him.
Even some of Paul’s friends who were officials of the province of Asia sent word to him, begging him not to venture into the theatre.
Meanwhile the assembly was in turmoil. Some were shouting one thing and some another, and most of them did not even know why they were there.
The Jews in the crowd pushed Alexander forward to explain himself, and he motioned for silence so he could make his defense to the people.
But when they realized that he was a Jew, they all shouted in unison for about two hours: “Great is Artemis of the Ephesians!”

Finally the city clerk quieted the crowd and declared, “Men of Ephesus, doesn’t everyone know that the city of Ephesus is guardian of the temple of the great Artemis and of her image, which fell from heaven?
Since these things are undeniable, you ought to be calm and not do anything rash.
For you have brought these men here, though they have neither robbed our temple nor blasphemed our goddess.
So if Demetrius and his fellow craftsmen have a complaint against anyone, the courts are open and proconsuls are available. Let them bring charges against one another there.
But if you are seeking anything beyond this, it must be settled in a legal assembly.
For we are in jeopardy of being charged with rioting for today’s events, and we have no justification to account for this commotion.”
After he had said this, he dismissed the assembly.

metanoia.gloss: Repentance, a turning of the mind and life. John's baptism was a baptism of metanoia that pointed forward to the Coming One. It was genuine but preparatory; it needed completion in faith in Jesus and the gift of the Spirit, which is why the Ephesian disciples are baptized into His name.

hodos.gloss: A road or way. In Acts the Way becomes an early name for the Christian faith, the whole life of following Jesus who is Himself the way. At Ephesus the Way is both maligned by opponents and threatened by a riot, yet the word of the Lord prevails against every obstacle in its path.

dynameis.gloss: Mighty works, acts of power, the plural of dynamis. God worked extraordinary dynameis through Paul's hands, but the power is God's, not Paul's or a relic's. The contrast with the sons of Sceva shows that such power belongs only to the name of the Lord Jesus and cannot be borrowed by the faithless.

logos.gloss: Word, message. Luke repeatedly makes the logos of the Lord the true hero of Acts: it spreads, grows, and prevails. At Ephesus, against magic and idolatry alike, the word of the Lord powerfully spreads and triumphs, showing that the gospel advances by God's message, not by spectacle.

basic.context: The third journey settles at Ephesus. First Apollos, mighty in Scripture but knowing only John's baptism, is taught more accurately by Priscilla and Aquila. Then Paul meets disciples who have not received the Spirit; he explains the gospel, baptizes them into Jesus, and the Spirit comes. For two years Paul preaches in the hall of Tyrannus until all Asia hears. The name of Jesus is honoured when the sons of Sceva fail, magic books are burned, and finally the silversmiths riot to defend Artemis.
basic.hermeneutics: Read the chapter as a series of contests between the name of the Lord Jesus and every rival power: partial knowledge, counterfeit exorcism, occult magic, and idol worship tied to money. The key question the Ephesian disciples raise is how and when the Spirit is received. The refrain to watch is that the word of the Lord grows and prevails.
basic.originalLanguages: John's baptism was one of metanoia, repentance, pointing to Christ. Opponents malign the hodos, the Way, yet God works dynameis, mighty acts, through Paul that no one can counterfeit. Above all, the logos, the word, of the Lord spreads and prevails, the true triumph of the chapter over magic and idolatry.
basic.typology: The burning of the magic books pictures the decisive break with the old life that faith in Christ demands. The powerlessness of the sons of Sceva against the demon shows that the name of Jesus is no magic formula but belongs to those who truly know Him. Artemis, the great goddess who seems to rule the city, is exposed as a man-made god who is no god at all.
basic.christ: The name of the Lord Jesus is the theme. It is honoured when counterfeit users fail, confessed when believers turn from magic, and defended by God against the idol trade. His name carries the Spirit's power and cannot be borrowed by the faithless.
basic.god: God pours out the Spirit, works mighty deeds, purges the church of magic, and overrules a riot through a pagan town clerk. He is sovereign over false religion and false power, and His word cannot be stopped by a city's wealth or worship.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians, written to this church, warns against the powers and calls believers to stand in Christ's armor. 1 Corinthians was written from Ephesus. The Spirit coming with tongues echoes Pentecost in Acts 2. The burning of magic books recalls the call to turn wholly from idols.
basic.oneStory: The gospel confronts the pagan world at its centre of religion and magic. The one story of Scripture, God claiming the nations for His Son, advances as the name of Jesus overthrows every rival at Ephesus and the word of the Lord prevails, pressing on toward Rome and the ends of the earth.

context.literary: A richly episodic chapter, Luke's fullest picture of an urban ministry, structured around the triumph of the name of Jesus and punctuated by his summary refrain that the word of the Lord grew and prevailed. The riot scene is one of Luke's most vivid, almost theatrical, set pieces.
context.historical: Ephesus was a leading city of the province of Asia, home to the temple of Artemis, one of the seven wonders of the ancient world, and a hub of magic; the Ephesian letters were famous magical texts. The city's identity and economy were bound up with the goddess, which is why the gospel's success threatened both the cult and the silversmiths' trade.
context.canonical: This ministry founded one of the most important churches of the New Testament, addressed in Paul's letter to the Ephesians and the site of much of his labor. The account displays the outward spread of the gospel and its inward power to purge idolatry and occultism from the lives of those who believe.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show the power of the name of the Lord Jesus, through the Spirit and the preached word, to overcome partial knowledge, counterfeit spirituality, occult power, and entrenched idolatry, so that the gospel takes root at the pagan heart of Asia and the word of the Lord prevails.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The unusual features, tongues at the laying on of hands, healing cloths, and the failed exorcism, are descriptive of a unique transitional moment as the gospel reached groups on the fringe of John's ministry. The abiding truths are prescriptive: the Spirit is given to those who believe in Jesus, His name is not a magic charm, and true faith breaks decisively with the occult.

debates.question: What do the Ephesian disciples and their rebaptism teach about receiving the Holy Spirit?

views.label: They were not yet Christians; the Spirit comes with true faith in Jesus
views.case: They knew only John's baptism and had not even heard of the Spirit, so they stood on the far side of the gospel, like disciples of John awaiting the Coming One. Paul completes their understanding, they believe in Jesus and are baptized into His name, and only then does the Spirit come. This reads the episode as a unique transitional case, not a second blessing, and it fits the New Testament pattern that all who believe in Christ receive the Spirit, Romans 8:9.

views.label: A subsequent reception of the Spirit after believing
views.case: Others take them as believers who received the Spirit in a distinct, later moment through the laying on of hands, and see here a pattern for a Spirit-reception beyond conversion. Rightly weighed, their ignorance of the Spirit and their rebaptism into Jesus mark them as not yet fully Christian, so the passage bridges John's era to the church rather than teaching a normal two-stage experience.

debates.bearing: The episode guards the truth that to belong to Christ is to have His Spirit; it addresses people caught between John's preparatory baptism and full faith in Jesus, and should not be pressed into a routine of Spirit-baptism separated from conversion.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter's key words trace the contest and its outcome. John's baptism was a baptism of metanoia, repentance, real but incomplete without faith in Jesus and the Spirit. Opponents slander the hodos, the Way, and later a riot rages over it, yet God works dynameis, mighty acts, through Paul that the sons of Sceva cannot imitate, proving the power is God's alone. Above all, Luke's verdict is that the logos, the word, of the Lord spreads and prevails, so that the true victor at Ephesus is not a miracle-worker but the message of Christ.

typology.type: The burning of the magic books
typology.fulfillment: The decisive renunciation of the old life that conversion to Christ demands, a putting off of the works of darkness, Ephesians 5:11, Romans 13:12.

typology.type: The Spirit coming on the Ephesian disciples
typology.fulfillment: The Pentecost gift extended to those on the edges of John's ministry, showing the one Spirit given to all who believe in Jesus, Acts 2 and Joel 2.

typology.type: Artemis, the great goddess exposed as no god
typology.fulfillment: The idols the prophets mocked, now unmasked by the gospel that man-made gods are no gods at all, Isaiah 44 and Jeremiah 10.

study.christ: Everything turns on the name of the Lord Jesus. His name, wrongly borrowed by the sons of Sceva, cannot be wielded as a charm and turns their attempt to disaster, so that His name is held in high honor. His name is the ground of the Spirit's coming, the confession of those who burn their magic, and the target of the riot's fury. Christ, though unseen in the tumult, is the one whose word and Spirit are conquering the city, and whose name every rival power must finally yield to.
study.god: God is sovereign over every power that claims the city. He gives His Spirit, works mighty deeds through Paul, humbles the counterfeit exorcists, moves believers to renounce the occult, and even restrains a deadly riot through the shrewd words of a pagan clerk. Against the temple of Artemis, the trade that depends on her, and the magic that enslaved many, God shows that His word cannot be silenced by wealth, religion, or mob. His purpose, that all Asia should hear, is accomplished.

crossReferences.note: Written to this church, calling believers to stand against the spiritual powers in the strength of the Lord, the very powers confronted at Ephesus.
Anyone who does not have the Spirit of Christ does not belong to Him, illuminating the case of the Ephesian disciples.
Pentecost, where the Spirit comes with tongues, echoed as the Spirit falls on the Ephesian disciples.
The folly of man-made gods, exposed by Paul's preaching that idols are no gods at all, which provokes the silversmiths.

study.oneStory: At Ephesus, a city of temple and magic, the name of the Lord Jesus overthrows every rival, and the word of the Lord prevails against half-knowledge, counterfeit power, the occult, and idolatry bound to money. In the sweep of Scripture this is God reclaiming the nations for His Son, unmasking their idols and filling His people with His Spirit, as the gospel presses on from Jerusalem toward Rome and the ends of the earth.

quiz.q: Why did Paul question and rebaptize the disciples he met at Ephesus?

options.text: They knew only John's baptism and had not received the Spirit, so he led them to full faith in Jesus.
options.note: They stood between John's preparatory baptism and full faith; Paul completed their understanding, baptized them into Jesus, and the Spirit came.

options.text: Their first baptism was invalid and had to be repeated correctly.
options.note: There is a real deficiency, but the issue was that John's baptism pointed forward and they needed faith in Jesus and His Spirit, not a mere technical repeat.

options.text: They had sinned and needed to be baptized again for forgiveness.
options.note: The issue was not fresh sin but that they were still on the far side of the gospel, knowing only John's baptism.

options.text: It teaches that every believer must be baptized twice.
options.note: This is a unique transitional case, not a rule that all believers be baptized twice.

quiz.q: What happened when the sons of Sceva tried to use the name of Jesus?

options.text: The evil spirit overpowered them, and as a result the name of Jesus was held in high honor.
options.note: They were beaten and fled, and fear and honor for the name of Jesus spread through the city.

options.text: They failed because they did not truly know Jesus.
options.note: True, they lacked real faith, but the fuller point is that their failure led to the name of Jesus being honored and the word prevailing.

options.text: They succeeded at first but later fell away.
options.note: They did not succeed at all; the demon turned on them immediately.

options.text: Nothing happened, since the name of Jesus has no power.
options.note: The opposite: the episode magnified the power of Jesus' name over the demon and the counterfeiters.

quiz.q: What does Luke repeatedly present as the true victor at Ephesus?

options.text: The word of the Lord, which powerfully spread and prevailed.
options.note: Luke's refrain is that the word of the Lord grew and prevailed over magic and idolatry alike.

options.text: The miracles God worked through Paul's hands.
options.note: The miracles were real and striking, but Luke's stated verdict is that the word of the Lord prevailed, not the miracles as such.

options.text: Paul's personal courage in facing the mob.
options.note: Paul was bold, but the disciples kept him from the theatre, and the triumph Luke names is the word of the Lord.

options.text: The town clerk who calmed the riot.
options.note: The clerk restrained the crowd, but he is not presented as the victor; the word of the Lord is.

quiz.q: Why did Demetrius stir up the silversmiths against Paul?

options.text: Paul's preaching that idols are no gods threatened both the cult of Artemis and their income.
options.note: Demetrius names both the loss of trade and the discrediting of Artemis as the danger.

options.text: He was defending the honor of the goddess Artemis.
options.note: He does appeal to Artemis, but Luke shows his first concern was the threat to their prosperity.

options.text: Paul had robbed the temple of Artemis.
options.note: The town clerk explicitly says they had not robbed the temple or blasphemed the goddess.

options.text: The Roman authorities had ordered him to arrest Paul.
options.note: No such order was given; Demetrius stirred up the riot himself over money and the cult.

quiz.q: How did the Ephesian believers show that their faith broke with their past?

options.text: They confessed their deeds and publicly burned their costly magic books.
options.note: Many came forward, confessed, and burned magic books worth fifty thousand drachmas.

options.text: They stopped attending the temple of Artemis.
options.note: Turning from idolatry is implied, but the concrete act Luke records is the confession and burning of magic books.

options.text: They gave away all their money to the poor.
options.note: They destroyed valuable magic books, but the text does not say they gave all their money to the poor.

options.text: They kept their magic quietly in case it was still useful.
options.note: The opposite: they burned the books openly, making a decisive, costly break.

questions: Priscilla and Aquila took Apollos aside and taught him more accurately in private. Who has quietly discipled you, and whom might you help grow without shaming them?
The Ephesian believers burned costly magic books to break with their old life. What would a decisive, even costly, break with your past look like as you follow Christ?
The sons of Sceva tried to use the name of Jesus without knowing Him. Where might you be treating faith as a formula or a technique rather than a real trust in the living Christ?
For two years the word of the Lord spread through patient, daily teaching in a lecture hall. How does that pattern reshape your expectations of how the gospel usually advances?
A whole city rioted to defend a god tied to its wealth and pride. What idols in your own culture, or your own heart, feel threatened when Jesus is truly Lord?

translationNotes.note: The interior renders a phrase that can also be given as the highland, describing Paul's overland route to Ephesus. Footnote markers in the source were omitted when building the verse text.
Fifty thousand drachmas, or pieces of silver, represents an enormous sum, since a drachma was roughly a day's wage; the figure shows the costly, decisive break the new believers made with the occult.

The source punctuates a clause with a dash before she who is worshiped by all the province of Asia; this study renders it with a comma to keep the sense while avoiding a dash, with no change of meaning.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Acts 18 and 19. Verse text for Acts 18:23-19:41 built by concatenating string and text elements with single spaces; footnote and lineBreak objects skipped. No verses are omitted in this range. A dash in Acts 19:27 was converted to a comma; no em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon verified this session.
